steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The researcher claims the ten-year-old survey no longer reflects today's residents' feelings. To support this, we need evidence that something relevant to walking behavior has changed in the neighborhood over the past ten years. The passage names sidewalks, crosswalks, and weather as the key factors influencing how far people will walk.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — sidewalks and crosswalks have increased substantially over the past ten years: The infrastructure factors the passage links directly to walking behavior have changed in this neighborhood since the survey was taken — exactly the evidence needed to show the old data is outdated. ✓
  • Choice B — confirms a factor without showing change: Describes an existing condition rather than a shift over the past ten years, so it doesn't prove the old survey is now inaccurate. ✗
  • Choice C — unrelated variable: Addresses something the passage does not identify as a factor influencing walking behavior. ✗
  • Choice D — unrelated satisfaction metric: Resident satisfaction with something other than walkability doesn't bear on whether the survey is outdated. ✗
That matches choice A — A is correct.
